coredata.dc:title: High photocatalytic performance of carbon quantum dots/TNTs composites for enhanced photogenerated charges separation under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Fenfen
Rong, Yuefei
Wan, Junmin
Hu, Zhiwen
Peng, Zhiqin
Wang, Bing
coredata.dc:description: A novel high-efficiency composite photocatalyst, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are embedded onto out-surface surface of the TiO2 nanotubes (TNTs) with high percentage of photocatalytic reactive facets, was fabricated through an improved hydrothermal method. The characterization results showed that the CQDs was chemically adhered to out-surface of the TNTs through the CO and/or CO bonds formed between the CQDs and TNTs. The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared photocatalysts was evaluated to degrade methylene blue (MB) solution under the visible light. The as-prepared photocatalysts had a marvelous photocatalytic performance and the CQDs/TNTs-0.2 sample had the best photocatalytic performance with a decolorization rate of 91.3% and approximately 2 times more than that of pure TNTs for the high initial concentration of MB solution (30 mg/l) within 50 min under visible-light irradiation. The CQDs with excellent Up-Conversion Photoluminescence(UCPL)properties as co-catalysts can covert long wavelength light (>600 nm) absorbed from infrared light into visible light (<600 nm), which activates the TNTs to produce electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the CQDs also can capture the photoexcited electrons and prolong the lifetime of the photoexcited charges. In addition, the stacking interaction of the π-π bonds between the CQDs and the dyes of the benzene ring structure would be beneficial for enhancing the absorption performance of the CQDs/TNTs photocatalysts. The photocatalytic mechanism and process have also been discussed in detail according to the characterization and photodegradation results. This work is of great interest to introduce a new visible-light photocatalyst for the treatment of organic wastewater and protect environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
TiO2 nanotube
Up-convension property
Charge separation
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from Zhejiang Top Priority Discipline of Textile Science and Engineering, Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LY13B030009 ), Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (ZSTU) (No. 1101820-Y ) and Zhejiang Provincial Top Key Academic Discipline of Chemical Engineering and Technology .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of the pure TNTs sample (a, b) and the CQDs/TNTs-0.2 sample (c, d).
EDS images and the elements weight of pure TNTs and the CQDs/TNTs-0.2 samples.
The XRD spectrogram of the pure TNTs and CQDs/TNTs-0.2 samples.
The FT-IR spectrogram of pure TNTs and the CQDs/TNTs samples.
The XPS spectrogram of pure TNTs and CQDs/TNTs 0.2 samples.
The UV–vis spectrogram of pure TNTs and CQDs/TNTs-0.2 samples.
The PL spectrogram of pure TNTs and CQDs/TNTs-0.2 samples.
DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra with the (a)OH; (b) O2 −; (c) whole record of the samples under the visible light.

(a) The degradation rate of MB as the concentration changes of the dye solution; (b) The degradation rate of MB by the compound as the concentration of the sample; (c) The UV scan spectrogram of the dye solution at different time.
Photocatalytic mechanism of the CQDs/TNTs photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: High photocatalytic performance of carbon quantum dots/TNTs composites for enhanced photogenerated charges separation under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Lab of Textile Fiber Materials & Processing Technology, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Lab of Textile Fiber Materials & Processing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Eco-Dyeing and Finishing of Textiles, Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou, 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Eco-Dyeing and Finishing of Textiles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel high-efficiency composite photocatalyst, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are embedded onto out-surface surface of the TiO2 nanotubes (TNTs) with high percentage of photocatalytic reactive facets, was fabricated through an improved hydrothermal method. The characterization results showed that the CQDs was chemically adhered to out-surface of the TNTs through the CO and/or CO bonds formed between the CQDs and TNTs. The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared photocatalysts was evaluated to degrade methylene blue (MB) solution under the visible light. The as-prepared photocatalysts had a marvelous photocatalytic performance and the CQDs/TNTs-0.2 sample had the best photocatalytic performance with a decolorization rate of 91.3% and approximately 2 times more than that of pure TNTs for the high initial concentration of MB solution (30 mg/l) within 50 min under visible-light irradiation. The CQDs with excellent Up-Conversion Photoluminescence(UCPL)properties as co-catalysts can covert long wavelength light (>600 nm) absorbed from infrared light into visible light (<600 nm), which activates the TNTs to produce electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the CQDs also can capture the photoexcited electrons and prolong the lifetime of the photoexcited charges. In addition, the stacking interaction of the π-π bonds between the CQDs and the dyes of the benzene ring structure would be beneficial for enhancing the absorption performance of the CQDs/TNTs photocatalysts. The photocatalytic mechanism and process have also been discussed in detail according to the characterization and photodegradation results. This work is of great interest to introduce a new visible-light photocatalyst for the treatment of organic wastewater and protect environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Situation of energy crisis and environment pollution urgently need to probe a kind of cost-effective clean energy. Solar energy is the most promising one of the cost-effective and renewable energy sources. The significant efforts have been made to convert solar energy into chemical energy using various semiconductor photocatalysts [1,2].

In recent years, carbon materials have attracted much attention because of its excellent characteristics. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) as atypical functional carbonaceous material [3] have some excellent characteristics such as low toxicity, luminescence [4,5], good up-converting and low cost [6,7]. Since 2004, as a new member of the nanocarbon family, great efforts have been made to synthesize the CQDs and expend its actual usage in different fields [8–11]. Normally, the CQDs are acquired by two routes including the top-down route [12–14] and the bottom-up route, respectively [15–18], and have been utilized in the bioimaging [19,20] and sensor [21]. In addition, the CQDs can capture the photoexcited electrons and prolong the lifetime of the photogenerated charges, the excellent UCPL properties of the CQDs as co-catalysts can covert long wavelength light (>600 nm) absorbed from visible light into shorter wavelength light (<600 nm). So, a number of researchers have attempted to use the CQDs combining with the semiconductor photocatalysts to expend the use of sunlight [22–25]. Zheng et al. has reported the TiO2 nanotube arrays was loaded by CQDs for the hydrogen generation under visible light [26]. Zhang et al. prepared the Fe2O3/carbon quantum dots nanocomposites to degradate the toxic gas [27]. What’s more, the CQDs combined with metal were used for the CO2 conversion and the photogeneration of H2 from H2O [28,29].

The photocatalytic method is becoming more and more popular among the classical routes of organic wastewater treatment. Many semiconductors such as the TiO2 [30], MoS2 [30] and ZnS [31] have been widely applied as photocatalysts because of their long-term chemical stability, good photostability, low production and low cost [32,33]. The TiO2 as a typical photocatalyst has been widely used because of the astounding performance in the photocatalysis. The TiO2 (Degussa P25) has many advantages such as high activity and long-term stability has been used commercially. The P25 with the mixed phase consisting of 70–80% anatase and 30–20% rutile is remarkable for the photocatalysis. Unfortunately, the P25 has a poor response to visible light because of its wide band gap (Eg = 3.2 eV). Moreover, the photoexcited electron-hole pairs of the P25 are very easy to combine [34]. These drawbacks greatly limit its practical usage. But many measures have taken to solve these problems by using the other nanomaterials as co-catalyst, which can greatly reduce band gap of the modified TiO2 and enhance its photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation [35]. To take full advantages of carbon materials and the TiO2, Cheng et al. prepared a stable and high-efficient visible-light photocatalyst of the TiO2/carbon core-shell nanofibers [36]. Saud et al. has also reported a hierarchical CQDs@TiO2 nanofiber to degradate the wastewater [8]. One-dimensional semiconductor nanomaterials extensively used are because of their large surface-to-volume ratio and the direct path for the diffusion of photoexcited electrons and holes [37,38].

Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were chemically embedded onto the external surface of the TNTs with high percentage of photocatalytic reactive facets through the CO and/or CO bonds formed between the CQDs and TNTs. The as-prepared samples had a marvelous photocatalytic performance for the high initial concentration of MB solution (30 mg/l) within 50 min under visible-light irradiation. The excellent photocatalytic performances are mainly due to the up-convention PL property of the CQDs, which can covert long wavelength light (>600 nm) absorbed from visible light into shorter wavelength light (<600 nm), and activate the TNTs to produce electron-hole pairs. In addition, the stacking interaction of the π–π bonds between the CQDs and the dyes of the benzene ring structure would be beneficial for improving the absorption performance of the CQDs/TNTs photocatalysts for the dye molecules of the benzene ring structure. This work is of great interest to introduce a new visible-light photocatalyst for the treatment of organic wastewater and protect environment.

TiO2 nanoparticles (P25, d = 21 nm) were purchased from Evonik-Degussa Co., Ltd., Germany. Citric acid and ethylene diamine was analytical reagent grade quality and purchased from Shanghai Entrepreneur Science & Trading. MB was also the analytical reagent grade quality and purchased from J&K. 1,2-Diaminoethane anhydrous and citric acid was purchased from Beijing Entrepreneur Science & Trading Co., China. Other chemical were analytical and without further purification. All these solution were prepared by high-purity deionized (DI) water.

In a typical procedure, a total of 2 g P25 was dispersed in 50 ml 10 M NaOH solution. The mixture solution was kept stirring for 50 min to ensure complete uniformity, then transferred into a 100-ml Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 135 °C for 36 h. The sediment of the mixture solution was treated with 0. 1 M HCl solution several times until the pH value of the solution was 1.5. The mixture solution was agitated overnight with a magnetic stirrer at room temperature, and then washed with the DI water several times until the pH value of the solution was 7. The sediment was dried at 80 °C for 24 h.

The solution of the CQDs was prepared according to the literature [39]. In a typical process as following: 3.1521 g of the citric acid and 1.005 ml of the ethylenediamine were put into 30 ml deionized water. Then the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and kept at 150 °C 5 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The brown-black aqueous solution was acquired and then on dialysis for two days. The solution of the CQDs was acquired and kept at 4 °C before using.

A different content (0.025 g, 0.05 g, 0.1 g, 0.2 g) of TNTs was dispersed in 20 ml CQDs solution, and kept stirring for 24 h. And they were marked as CQDs/TNT-0.025, CQDs/TNT-0.05, CQDs/TNT-0.1, CQDs/TNT-0.2, respectively. Then, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 10000 rmp/min for 30 min. Then, the final products were dried at 70 °C for 12 h.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and HR-TEM images were obtained from the JEOL JEM-2010-HT instrument and the accelerating voltage was 200 kV to characterize the microscope of the as-prepared samples. The crystal structures of the as-prepared samples were analyzed using the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). The X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation, the accelerating voltage was 40 kV and the applied current was 44 mA, with the scanning rate of 3°/min−1 in the 2ϴ range from 10° to 90° by using a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer. The TEM-mapping was used to analysis the elements of the samples. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker to analize the states of the different functional groups of the as-prepared samples. Then, the chemical states of the elements of the as-prepared samples was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS,PHI-5300 ESCA, PerkinElmer, Boston, MA). UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded through the UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary 500) equipped with an integrating scope assembly which the scanning range was of 250–650 nm. The active species of the samples were evaluated by PL and EPR.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was evaluated by photodegradating MB aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. The proper concentration of the dye solution is very important for the photocatalytic efficiency and process. So, 0.01 g of the CQDs/TNTs was added into the 20 mg/l, 30 mg/l, and 40 mg/l of the MB solution (30 ml), respectively. According to the photocatalytic results of the different concentration of MB solution, 30 mg/l of MB solution concentration was chosen as the proper dye solution. Then, 0.01 g CQDs/TNTs composite was put into a 30 ml of 30 mg/l MB aqueous solution. Before each test, the suspension was always kept stirring for 30 min to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the cycling water and an exhaust fan were used to keep the temperature balance of the system and avoid the influence of the environment. Under visible light irradiation, 4 ml of the suspension solution was periodically extracted from the reaction cell at given time intervals (10 min) by centrifugation at 8000 rmp/min for 5 min. The concentration of MB solution was recorded by the UV–vis spectrometer at a maximum wavelength of 660 nm.

The HR-TEM has been used to obtain the microstructure of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the one-dimensional TNTs have a quite smooth surface structure and its diameter is about 8 nm on average for the external diameter, the wall thickness is 2 nm. Fig. 1c and d show the corresponding high magnification TEM images of the CQDs/TNT-0.2 sample. The surface of the TNTs become rough after coated with CQDs. The CQDs are about 4 nm in size and dispersed on the surface of the TNTs disorderly. The lattice fringes with d spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm all agree well with the TiO2 (101) plane and CQDs (100) plane, respectively [7]. The results of Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) further confirm the formation of the CQDs/TNTs. In Fig. 2 , there is no C element in the TNTs, but the CQDs/TNTs-0.2 sample consists of C, O and Ti elements. The results also confirm the existence of the CQDs in the CQDs/TNTs samples.

XRD patterns in Fig. 3 clearly show the TNTs with a mixed phase containing the anatase and rutile phase, and also confirm the TNTs has a higher photocatalytic efficiency [37,40]. As can be shown that the TNTs and the CQDs/TNTs samples have the same typical diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 25.1°, 28.2°, 39.39°, 48.42°, which indicate that the crystalline of the TiO2 structure is not be changed after modification by the CQDs. The peak at 2θ = 25.1° is in line with the (101) facet which matches the lattice spacing of 0.34 nm [41,42]. Furthermore, both of the samples have the same peaks at 2θ = 28.22° and 32.3°, which perfectly index to the (R101) and (R110) of the TiO2. The detected peaks at 48.32° is assigned to (105) planes of the TiO2 [43]. Moreover, the typical peaks of the carbon isn’t detected maybe because the content of the CQDs are too low in the as-prepared samples [8,44].

To confirm the combination between the TNTs and CQDs, the functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared samples were characterized by FT-IR. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the CQDs/TNTs and the TNTs samples display similar spectra, which indicate that they all have the comparable skeletons. The absorption bands at 3600 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of the TiOH groups on the out-surface of the TNTs [45], which can capture the photoexcited electrons and holes to produce the reactive oxygen species in the photocatalytic process of organic wastewater [46,47]. The peak at 1620 cm−1 are corresponding to the surface adsorbed water molecules [48] and the bands at 1390 cm−1 and 1156 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COC groups [49]. All these results confirm that the CQDs and TNTs have a good chemical connection.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained to evaluate the surface chemical composition and binding energy of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 5 , there are four spectrograms of the O, Ti, C and the total image. As can be seen from Fig. 5 (a), the O 1 s has three typical peaks located at 530.4 eV, 531.4 eV and 532.4 eV. The lowest binding energy is assigned to the TiO bond. The peaks at 531.4 eV and 532.4 eV are ascribed to the CO and OH bonds, respectively [50]. The results suggest that the TNTs have already been modified by the CQDs. Furthermore, there are both two peaks of the Ti element of the pure TNTs and the as-prepared samples in Fig. 4 (b), the peaks located at 464.68 eV and 458.95 eV are ascribed to the Ti (2p1/2) and Ti (2p3/2) of the pure TNTs. But the binding energies of the Ti (2p1/2) and Ti (2p3/2) of the CQDs/TNTs-0.2 sample located at 465.28 eV and 459.58 eV slightly shift and are higher than that of the pure TNTs. The results show that the chemical environment of Ti element has been changed because of the formation of TiOC bond between the CQDs and the TNTs in the CQDs/TNTs samples. The C (1s) XPS spectra of the as-prepared samples show in Fig. 4 (c), the typical peaks at 284.9 eV, 286.5 eV and 289.2 eV are consistent with the CC, CO and OCO bonds, respectively. The results further confirm the presence of CQDs in the as-prepared samples. Notably, these results also reveal that the chemical states of the C, O and Ti elements of the as-prepared samples still have not been changed when the TNTs were modified by CQDs, and are still consistent with the results of XRD.

Absorption performance of the as-prepared samples was evaluated by the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) under the visible light. As seen from Fig. 6 . The three curves have a significant absorption below 350 nm, which are ascribed to the intrinsic band gap of the TNTs. The absorption activity of the CQDs/TNTs slightly enhances under visible light irradiation compared with the pure TNTs, which reveals that the CQDs can expand the CQDs/TNTs photocatalyst into the visible-light absorption scope because of the UCPL property of the CQDs. The results also confirm that the CQDs play an important role in the degradation process of the dye solution and make the CQDs/TNTs samples utilize more visible light.

Photoluminescence (PL) techniques were used to evaluate the transfer and recombination process of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs. In Fig. 7 , the TNTs are excited by 385 nm wavelength light to bring two typical emission peaks located at 390 nm and 466 nm, respectively, but the peaks of the CQDs/TNTs samples are obviously lower than that of the pure TNTs. The results confirm that the electron-hole pairs have been separated more efficiency, the CQDs in the samples play the role of an electron receptor to capture electrons and impede the recombination of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs, which results in the decrease of PL intensity.

Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) technique was performed to investigate the active species during the photocatalytic process by using the DMPO as the trapping agent. As seen from Fig. 8 (a), under visible light irradiation, the strong peaks of the spectrogram of OH are ascribed to the CQDs/TNTs samples and the weak peaks are ascribed to the pure TNTs, which indicate that more OH active species are generated on the surface of the TNTs modified by CQDs. The O− 2 active species generated have the similar results with the OH active species. Fig. 8 (c) shows the whole spectrogram of the pure TNTs and the CQDs/TNTs samples. The peak intensity of the O− 2 radicals are much higher than that of the OH radicals, which indicates the O− 2 radicals are greatly more than the OH radicals in the photocatalytic process under visible light irradiation. The results also reveal that the O− 2 radicals play the typical factors in the whole photocatalytic process under visible light irradiation, and the CQDs play an important role in the photocatalytic process because the CQDs accelerate transfer of the photogenerated charges and retard the recombination of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs.

The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared samples is evaluated to degradate MB solution under visible light irradiation. The MB solution containing catalysts was put in the dark environment and kept stirring for 30 min to obtain the adsorption equilibrium before visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic activities of different concentrations of MB solution were showed in Fig. 9 (a). The as-prepared samples have a perfect absorption for the MB solution. The curves vividly show that the concentration of the MB solution has sharp decline within 20 min in the dark environment. But the degradation rates of 10 mg/l, 20 mg/l and 30 mg/l MB solution are more than 99%, 99% and 91.3% after visible light irradiation, respectively. So, the concentration of the 30 mg/l MB solution was chosen as a suitable concentration to evaluate the photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 9 (b), the CQDs/TNTs samples exhibit the highest photocatalytic activity with degradation rate of 91.3% for the 30 mg/l MB solution, which is 2 times more than that of pure TNTs (45.6%). The increasing of the TNTs contents results in the gradual increasing of the photocatalytic degradation efficiency, which can be explained that the heterojunction is formed between the CQDs and the TNTs [51]. The photodegradation products of MB molecules were evaluated by using the ultraviolet spectrophotometer. As can be seen from Fig. 9 (c), the peaks at 245 nm, 292 nm, 609 nm and 663 nm correspond to the characteristic absorption peaks of MB molecules. These peaks all become more and more weak as the photocatalytic reaction process proceeds. The results show that the special bond of MB molecule corresponding to the characteristic absorption peaks located at 663 nm has been destroyed. Moreover, the peaks located at 609 nm and 663 nm both slightly shift towards the long wavelength scope. The results also confirm that the structure of the reaction products have been changed compared with the original reagent.

As known to all, the pure TNTs are not an efficient photocatalyst because wide band gap of the pure TNT sonly absorbs short wavelength light (UV light) consisting of less than 5% of the sunlight. So, the CQDs are very crucial in the photodegradation process of the dye solution. Fig. 10 shows the photocatalytic mechanism of the CQDs/TNTs photocatalysts. As the carbon nanostructure has a huge capacity of electron storage, the CQDs can trap the electrons photoexcited from the TNTs, which retards the recombination of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, the electrons from the conduction band of the TNTs can react with the molecular oxygen to produce superoxide anion radical. At the same time, the photogenerated holes left in the valence band can also move onto the surface of the TNTs and react with H2O to form active hydroxyl radicals. The superoxide anion radicals and the hydroxyl radicals both have very strong oxidation capability to degrade the organic wastewater. In addition, the UCPL properties of the CQDs can transfer the long wavelength light of the near infrared region into the short wavelength light, which would excite the TNTs to create more electron-holes pairs. Moreover, the stacking interaction of the π-π bonds between the CQDs and the MB dyes of the benzene ring structure is beneficial for improving the absorption performance of the CQDs/TNTs photocatalysts for the MB molecules [9,27,52]. The photocatalytic mechanism under visible-light irradiation is illustrated as follows:
(1)

wavelength → CQDs + short
wavelength

A novel composite photocatalyst, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were embedded onto the external surface of the TiO2 nanotubes (TNTs), were fabricated through an improved hydrothermal method. The as-prepared samples were analyzed by TEM, EDS, FTIR and many other methods. The results indicate that the CQDs play an important role in the visible-light photocatalytic process. First of all, the electrons photogenerated from the TNTs can be trapped by the CQDs and retard the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. What’s more, the UCPL property of CQDs could make the CQDs/TNTs utilize visible light more efficiently [52]. The heterostructure formed between the CQDs and the TNTs can greatly prolong the life of the photoexcited electron and hole pairs [45]. So, this work is of great interest to introduce a new and high efficiency way to fabricate a visible-light photocatalyst and provide a useful thought for the treatment of organic wastewater and environment protection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from Zhejiang Top Priority Discipline of Textile Science and Engineering, Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LY13B030009), Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (ZSTU) (No. 1101820-Y) and Zhejiang Provincial Top Key Academic Discipline of Chemical Engineering and Technology.
